sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 1.8g of fat. polyunsaturated fat is one of the better parts of the profile. for a legume, this is exactly the kind of profile you want.
sections.subtitleText: 1.8g of fat. polyunsaturated fat is one of the better parts of the profile. for a legume, this is exactly the kind of profile you want.

sections.title: Carbs
sections.narration: 4g of carbs. fibre is where things start to fall off. for legumes, the carbs look much better when fibre and protein are both backing them up.
sections.subtitleText: 4g of carbs. fibre is where things start to fall off. for legumes, the carbs look much better when fibre and protein are both backing them up.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: 3.3g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. protein support is a big part of what makes legumes worth it.
sections.subtitleText: 3.3g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. protein support is a big part of what makes legumes worth it.

sections.narration: everything else here is pretty flat.

sections.narration: minerals are basically not part of the case here.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: on the plus side: filling for the calories because legumes usually earn a lot of practical value from satiety. cheap staple with real payoff because this matters because a food is stronger when people can actually use it often. fibre-forward category identity because legumes are supposed to bring stable carbs and fibre.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: now the downsides: dilution makes it less impressive than whole legumes because the legume identity is still there, but not in full strength. can take planning or prep because that is a small but real friction cost. protein quality still has a ceiling because good, but not meat or dairy level.

closing.summary: Unsweetened Soy Milk does have some solid upside. but the tradeoffs keep it from climbing higher. best when you want fibre, protein, and actual meal utility together.
